text: This product team is, yeah, basically. Basically the idea is like how to replicate the conditions that would be true if we're starting a company from scratch today. And yeah, it would be 996, it would be in person, it would be much more intense, right? And I think you'd be shipping at a much different pace than a larger company. Yeah, I think when you work inside of a company, oftentimes people want to work on the new shiny thing. So it's a good filter to be like, you can, but like this is the minimum, here's the bar, right? And I think there's a, we talk a lot about the, like, self-selection being a good thing. Like, some people just say, not for me. And we're like, great. Like, we, that's why we're explicit about it. Like, it's intense. It's long hours. It's a lot of grind. It's, like, relatively improved in technology. So there's a lot of, like, things that people haven't just built. You know, we had to build, like, for example, we build a, we had to do an entire framework for running evaluations on how agents collaborate to get tasks done. Actually, no one, that doesn't exist. There's a lot of tools for running evals on a prompt. Like, here's a prompt, a simple one. Is it doing what you expect? Yes, you know. But when you have sub-agents coordinating tasks across five or 10 or 15 different systems, it's very hard to test that. So we had to build that framework, right? We didn't know that when we started building this. Maybe to map that back to traditional dev work, it'd be like eval unit testing people have done. Exactly, integration testing. Integration eval testing. So I had to build a framework for that. And that's been something surprising. Actually, our CTO built a lot of the framework. We talk a lot about it. Are you going to open source your email? We actually probably will. Interesting. So there's been a lot of good work there. But, you know, that's the kind of intensity that's required. But they're like, yeah, there's a roadblock. No one's done this before. And you can sort of retract or lean in. And I think people that retract are probably not going to be fit. If you do open sources, I think that's great. It's also a great way, like, you see who's contributing to it. You're like, oh, maybe they'd be a good fit to come work on this at Brex. Yeah. Okay, so we've talked about the archetype of the team, some of the, like, commitment the team makes to the product. What about the actual skill set of the team? Are you like, we want, you know, the team is 30 people, and we want 29 of them to be engineers? Yeah, I think it's like 20. Right now it's probably 15 people, so it's probably going to be 20. We don't think we need hundreds. We don think we need even more than 30 probably just because we did a lot of things around you know actually building this new layer of Brex in agentic code base as well So we built a lot of developer infrastructure around making sure that each individual developer that is contributing is materially more productive. So we did a lot of sort of work behind the work on that regard to keep the head down small. The way we think about skills is like a lot of the people that tend to do well, they have been building of LLMs for a while. That's probably since before Chai GPT launched. So they just have more affinity with the way of dealing with non-deterministic technology, which is a lot of what LMs are. And I think there's also a fair amount of awareness of what makes these experiences good. So a lot of the quality controls happens on eValves, for example. A lot of quality controls happens on, is there a really hard problem that actually needs to use reinforcement learning? And there's a lot of things like that that I think we're starting to lean in more that folks that have been building these technologies for a longer time are good. Also, a lot of founders or ex-founders that had this what I call astronomical sense of agency, they just take far more initiative than any normal person would. So these are the traits, but really it's like a high technical bar. very strong product taste, very strong levels of ownership, and willing to endure a lot of intensity. Yeah. What are your intermediate success criteria? The end-state success criteria can often be easy. It's like, look, it's doing billions of revenue at a high gross margin, making our customers very happy. But three months into a project, you'd, like, define the problem, and maybe you get an MVP out in a month. And you have to decide, like, do I keep going? Do I have more resources, fewer resources? I think it's fairly simple. I think it's like, are the people building it proud of it? And I think the, you know, the, like, I like writing a lot, and there's a, I read a lot of the books about writing, and there's a good book on writing well. It talks about this idea that, you know, when you're writing something, you know, people worry a lot about the audience. Who's the audience? What are the audience going to think? And it's a very simple question with a very simple answer. The answer to the audience is you, right? So you're building it for yourself. And I think the people if you have the right people they tend to have a very good pulse on whether they proud of what they have what they built So I think the question you can ask people is like are you proud of this And typically it gives you a very good sense of, like, whether it's at the bar or not. And most of the times people tell you not and no, and then sometimes you have to be like, well, maybe you're being too harsh on yourself. But when they say yes, the bar is pretty much met. So I think that's a good one. So maybe you're also saying if you set the bar on hiring very high, then it makes it easier to determine success because they'll self-evaluate. Exactly, exactly. And the other one is just like early customer feedback. Like what are people saying when they start using it, onboarding? What are the outcomes it's driving for them, right? But this is pretty obvious. I think the early success is like what's the level of energy and enthusiasm and pride of people building it. Yeah. And talk to us a little bit about a scope of what that product is. I don't want to steal all the thunder, and I know you're going to have more to talk about later, but what are the types of problems you're working on, and what is the current footprint of the product? Yeah, I think we're building effectively the agent platform that will sit on top of every single Brex product and surface and effectively automate whatever process happened on that part of the product or part of the company. So you can think about it as like ingesting effectively a policy or an SOP or a set of guidelines from the company and doing most of that work automatically on behalf of a user. So that's the general principle. And we're shipping a set of agents first and then others early next year. But really, a lot of the way that we thought about it is the investment on the platform is really important because the challenge is, like, it's not as much, you know, how do you build something that works well in the demo? The challenge is, like, how do you get a chief county officer of a $100 billion company comfortable with this? Yeah. And that requires a lot of, like, product thinking. on like, and actually one of the things that we see a lot in AI is like people, most people that are working at this level and, you know, running finance in a company, they're actually fairly smart. And we believe a lot in this idea of like talking up to users, right? So instead of like dumping down concepts and saying, no, the AI did this for you automatically, like users actually gain a lot more confidence when they can go under the hood and see like, okay what are the evals that are actually running here What is this doing to my past data If I change this what happens to all past examples right So there enough context and sort of bringing folks along the journey that you can do in order to get to a really good outcome. So we're trying to get these details right because we think that is what's going to make a lot of the future agents we build much better. So that's a lot of where the time and energy is going now. Yeah. Yeah. You know, we think about scoping a new product inside of a company. So you've always said, like, look, same customers, new product, and then we build our own. Initially, right. Yeah, initially. And then, you know, building our own kind of independent interior team. And you've talked about what you find. Another question, too, is does that product have the same or a different business model? And so for you, is it like, OK, look, this is going to have the same business model as the core Brex platform? or are you going to have to think about changing your business model? Yeah, a lot of our business model is effectively, you know, the best way to describe it is we monetize customers like a bank, but we retain like a software company, we retain and differentiate like a software company, right? So that remains true. Like a lot of the way we monetize directly early on is, you know, through interchange and banking and FX and all these things. In the future, though, we think that the opportunity to actually start displacing labor is actually potentially even bigger. So the way to think about it is, the phase one of Brex was very focused on purely financial services, like card and banking. Phase two was very focused on... So interchange and flow. Exactly. Phase two was very focused on how do you add in software into a lot of these products and make them dramatically more compelling as part of a broader workflow that you have inside your company. And then replacing the sort of concurs the world and all that, right? And then the third wave is effectively how to actually replace the labor itself. And the business model relatively maps to each of these phases, although labor is not, we haven't yet started to charge for it because I think it's unclear what's the best way of doing it, but we're definitely experimenting. It's also unclear, even if it's huge on lock, I think a lot of times there's unwillingness to pay, even if you can demonstrate labor savings. Exactly. So a lot of it for us is something we're going to experiment on. But the reality is we're much more focused at this stage of the game in creating the value and capturing the value. So as long as we actually... if customers are saying,
